notes.note: The Latin dateline reads "D. xiii K. Apr.", meaning dispatched on the 13th day before the Kalends of April = 20 March 43 BC.

notes.anchor: your colleague, that most illustrious man, is being besieged
notes.note: Decimus Iunius Brutus Albinus (not the more famous Marcus Brutus) had been appointed governor of Cisalpine Gaul by Julius Caesar and held it for the senate after the assassination; he was besieged in Mutina (Modena) by Mark Antony from late 44 until April 43 BC.

M. Aemilius Lepidus, governor of Narbonese Gaul and Nearer Spain, had also written to the Senate advocating peace with Antony; both letters were received with deep suspicion by Cicero and the Senate's Optimate majority.

notes.anchor: the bonds of the times have linked you
notes.note: Cicero alludes diplomatically to Plancus's political situation: as a Caesar partisan and now consul-designate, Plancus had professional and personal ties to Antony's circle that were dictated by circumstance rather than by genuine sympathy.

notes.anchor: not merely a consul and a consular, but a great consul and a great consular too
notes.note: A consularis (consular) was a former consul, a permanent rank of prestige in the Senate; Cicero's point is that the mere title means nothing without the moral conduct that justifies it, pressing Plancus to act decisively for the optimates.
